data.title: Dollar General Remains A Discounted Play
data.description:
The last few months have not been a very good time for the market. Most companies have experienced a great deal of pain because of economic uncertainty brought on by tariffs and other trade related threats. However, one company that has held up nicely is none other than discount retailer Dollar General Corporation (NYSE:DG). In fact, since I upgraded the company to a ‘buy’ in January of this year, shares have skyrocketed by 22.5%. That's well above the 8.9% decline experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

The newest data provided by management shows that growth continues, though there have been some signs of weakness. Furthermore, even though shares are not as cheap as they were previously, they have remained attractively priced from a valuation standpoint. Expectations of continued growth on both the top and bottom lines indicate to me that the business should continue to fare well in the long run. So even though it might be a bumpy ride, I think that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate is logical at this point in time.

Shares are still on sale

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding Dollar General covers the last quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. According to management, sales for that window of time came in at $10.30 billion. That's 4.5% above the $9.86 billion the business reported just one year earlier. When you dig deeper into the data, you find that this was the result of two things. The primary contributor to this increase was a rise in the number of stores in operation from 19,986 to 20,594. That's a year-over-year growth of 3%.

On top of this, the company also saw same store sales growth of 1.3%. That compares to the 0.7% increase experienced in the final quarter of 2023 compared to the final quarter of 2022. This is where some of the weakness comes into play, though. While the firm did enjoy a 2.3% increase in the average transaction value at its stores, traffic actually contracted by 1.1% on a same store basis. This means that while overall sales are up, it is coming from fewer customers paying more. In this environment, which is defined already by a tight budgeted consumer, this is yet further evidence of weakness.

On the bottom line, the picture was more complicated. From the final quarter of 2023 to the final quarter of 2024, net profits for the company fell by more than half from $401.8 million to $191.2 million. This is in spite of the fact that a reduction in net debt from $6.62 billion to $5.31 billion, which I would say is a substantial reduction, allowed interest expense to fall from $77.2 million to $65.9 million. There was a slight contraction in the company's gross profit margin from 29.48% to 29.40%. But the real problem for it involved its selling, general, and administrative costs. Management claimed that the rise from 23.60% of sales to 26.55% was driven in part by higher retail labor costs, incentive compensation, repairs and maintenance expenses, depreciation and amortization, and technology related expenses. But the big problem here involved the $214 million of impairment charges associated with the company’s store portfolio optimization review.

Speaking of net debt, in the chart above you can see how it has declined over the last three years. That chart also shows other important balance sheet related metrics like Dollar General’s debt to equity ratio and its current ratio. While the latter of these has remained at the same level for two years now, the firm’s debt to equity ratio has improved thanks to the paydown of debt. This is certainly encouraging.

During the final quarter of last year, management initiated this review involving not only its flagship Dollar General branded stores, but also its pOpshelf bannered stores. This review culminated in the company deciding to close 96 Dollar General stores and 45 pOpshelf stores. It will keep six of its pOpshelf stores, but it will convert them into Dollar General stores, ultimately bringing an end to its pOpshelf concept. Total charges associated with this amounted to $232 million, $214 million of which involved impairment charges. It's unclear what the remaining $18 million of expenses fell under.

Net income was not the only part of the business to suffer. In fact, most profitability metrics worsened on a year-over-year basis. Operating cash flow fell from $949.8 million to $800.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $627.7 million to $666 million. However, EBITDA for the business worsened from $809.3 million to $774.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2024 fiscal year as a whole compared to 2023. Revenue jumped from $38.69 billion to $40.61 billion. Same store sales grew by 1.4%, driven mostly by a 1.1% rise in traffic and, to a lesser extent, by a 0.3% increase in its average transaction price. Despite this nice increase in revenue, the firm's bottom line worsened, plunging from $1.66 billion to $1.13 billion. A small portion of this was because of an uptick in shrinkage from $910.7 million to $928.9 million. For those not familiar with that term, shrinkage is essentially what management considers to be theft. Unfortunately, this has become a problem for the company in recent years. In 2022, the company had only $481 million in shrinkage. That amounted to 1.27% of sales. But by the end of 2024, it had increased to 2.29%. This may not seem like much. But when applied to the kind of revenue the company generated last year, it's an extra $414.2 million in pre-tax profits that investors are missing out on. Of course, the impairment charge that I mentioned already played a much larger role in the recent decline in profitability. As the aforementioned chart illustrates, all other profitability metrics, except for operating cash flow, worsened year over year in 2024.

When it comes to 2025, management stated that revenue should climb by between 3.4% and 4.4%. Part of this growth will be because of same store sales growth of between 1.2% and 2.2%. But the biggest portion of the increase should be attributable to the addition of 575 new US stores and up to 15 new stores in Mexico. This all compares to the set of 125 new stores opened system-wide last year. But that's not all. In what has been called Project Elevate, Dollar General is focused on revitalizing its brand. This will see partial remodelings of 2,250 of its stores. The company will also fully remodel 2,000 locations on top of relocating 45 existing stores. This translates to a total of 4,885 real estate projects that the company will be working on. For context, last year, in addition to the 725 stores opened, the firm remodeled 1,621 stores and relocated 85 others.

This increase in revenue for the company should make possible a rise in profitability as well. Management currently anticipates earnings per share of between $5.10 and $5.80. This would mean net profits of about $1.20 billion, compared to the $1.13 billion reported for 2024. If we assume that other profitability metrics will rise at the same rate as this, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $2.58 billion and EBITDA of $3.10 billion.

Using these estimates provided by management, and also using the historical figures that the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. Are multiples that I am generally bullish about. Furthermore, if you compare Dollar General to two other major discount retailers, as also shown in the aforementioned chart, you can see that our candidate is in the middle of the group when it comes to both the price to earnings approach and the price to operating cash flow approach. Only when it involves the EV to EBITDA multiple is Dollar General the most expensive of the three.

Longer term, management expects to achieve same store sales growth of between 2% and 3% per annum. Beginning next year, it also anticipates diluted earnings per share growth of 10% or greater. Using about 3% of net revenue effective this year, the company intends to grow its store count by around 2% annually. This will help, when combined with the aforementioned same store sales growth, the company in grow annual revenue by between 3.5% and 4%. Unfortunately, we could see some weakness because of broader economic concerns. In its final quarter earnings press conference for 2024, the management team at the enterprise did not really offer up much in the way of detail here. They did say that they believed that the firm is ‘well positioned to mitigate the impact’ of tariffs this year. They also stressed that they were able to successfully mitigate the tariff impacts that were endured in 2018 and 2019. It's unclear the extent to which the firm will have to raise prices for its customers. For instance, back in 2018 and 2019, the company did need to raise prices in some categories. However, management also acknowledged that its core customers are ‘already stressed’ from a financial perspective. And as such, the company is closely monitoring matters.

In the chart above, you can see share price performance for the enterprise since I last wrote about it earlier this year. At the start of this article, I mentioned that shares are up 22.5%. This chart shows an increase of 21.7%. This disparity is because of the timing of when my article was published compared to the timing used in the chart. But it's close enough. For context, that chart also shows how Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Five Below (FIVE) have performed over the same window of time. They have paled in comparison. This seems to indicate that the market is also currently in agreement with my opinion that Dollar General is perhaps the better candidate to keep in mind at this time. As other analysts have pointed out, including those at Citi Research, the company’s business model as one that focuses on delivering value for customers should help it to avoid significant pressure in this environment. When they decided to upgrade the company from a ‘sell’ to a ‘neutral’ (what Seeking Alpha designates as a ‘hold’), they did so while pointing out that only around 10% of the company's revenue is ultimately affected by tariffs. If true, this could help the company in many ways.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I am impressed with Dollar General. Honestly, I regret not buying shares for cheaper when I had the chance. Even though financial performance has been mixed as of late, with the bottom-line being particularly challenged, the long-term outlook is upbeat and shares are still trading on the cheap on an absolute basis. Given these factors, I have no problem keeping the company rated a ‘buy’ right now.

data.title: Evaluating Fibra UNO After Its Recent Outperformance
data.description:
Almost four months ago, I recommended holding Fibra UNO (OTCPK:FBASF) for its robust business model, its positive business momentum, and its exceptionally high dividend yield. I did not rate the stock as a “buy” due to its high debt load. Since that article, the stock has outperformed the market by a wide margin. More precisely, the stock has offered a total return of 4.5% whereas the S&P 500 and The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE) have declined 14% and 7%, respectively. After such striking outperformance, it is only natural to wonder whether the time has come for the shareholders of Fibra UNO to take their profits or if they should continue holding the stock.

Business Overview

Fibra UNO is the first and largest REIT in Mexico, with 613 properties in its portfolio.

Source: Investor Presentation for Q4-2024

Unlike other REITs, which are focused on one type of property, Fibra UNO is highly diversified. It generates 41% of its net operating income from retail properties, 33% from industrial properties, 14% from office properties and the remaining 12% from other properties.

The REIT reported its results for the fourth quarter in late February. Revenue grew 5% over the prior year’s quarter thanks to the growth of leasable area and rent hikes, which resulted from the pass-through of inflation to tenants. All the contracts of the company are indexed to inflation; this is paramount in the highly inflationary environment prevailing right now. Inflation in Mexico peaked at 5.6% a few months ago, and it is currently standing at 3.8%.

Moreover, Fibra UNO improved its occupancy rate sequentially from 95.3% to 95.6%, primarily thanks to an increase in the occupancy rate of retail properties from 92.7% to 93.7%. Notably, the industrial properties of the REIT had an impressive occupancy rate of 98.2%. The only weak point, as expected, was the occupancy of the office portfolio, which improved sequentially by 20 basis points but remained suppressed at 83.7%. Overall, thanks to improved occupancy rates and material rent hikes, Fibra UNO grew its net operating income and its funds from operations [FFO] per share by 3% and 10%, respectively, over the previous quarter.

The other major development since my previous article on the stock is the deteriorating economic landscape due to the aggressive tariffs that the new U.S. government has announced. China has already retaliated. If the other major trading partners of the U.S. retaliate too, then global economic growth is likely to be hurt.

On the bright side, Fibra UNO is much less affected than most companies, as it does not import any goods and hence it is not directly affected by tariffs. In addition, it is located in Mexico and hence it is less affected by tariffs than U.S. companies. This helps explain the striking outperformance of the stock vs. the S&P 500 and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF, both of which include U.S. companies.

On the other hand, the above does not mean that Fibra UNO is immune to a potential global recession. If the ongoing trade war accentuates, it may cause a global economic slowdown or a recession. In such a case, Fibra UNO will probably experience lower demand for its properties and some of its tenants may struggle to pay for their rent obligations. Nevertheless, the REIT is highly diversified by quantity and types of tenants. It does business with several defensive tenants, such as Unilever, Walmart, PepsiCo, AT&T, etc. It also has a strong position in its business, as it has a market share of 11% in the office market, 11.5% in the retail market, and 10% in the industrial market of Mexico. To cut a long story short, Fibra UNO is not immune, but it is more resilient than most companies to a potential global recession.

Dividend – Debt

Fibra UNO has paid $0.11 of dividends in the last 12 months. This amount of dividends corresponds to an exceptionally high yield of 10.5%. However, the AFFO payout ratio is currently standing at 84%, which is elevated even for a REIT.

In addition, the company has a weak balance sheet. It has a leverage ratio (net debt to EBITDA) of 7.0x and its net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) is $7.7 billion. This amount is nearly twice as much as the market capitalization of the stock ($4.1 billion) and hence it is excessive. Moreover, interest expense consumes 53% of operating income.

As long as Fibra UNO maintains its solid business performance, it is not likely to have any problem servicing its debt and paying its generous dividend. However, if the drama with tariffs escalates, it may result in a global slowdown or a recession. In such a case, Fibra UNO may cut its dividend. On the bright side, management recently stated that it intends to reduce the leverage ratio from 7.0x to approximately 5.5x in the long run. Nevertheless, until management delivers on its pledge, the REIT will be vulnerable to a future downturn in its business.

The only positive aspect of a recession is the fact that interest rates will probably decrease in such a scenario. As a result, the REIT will be able to refinance its debt at lower interest rates, and thus it will probably enjoy a significant decrease in its interest expense.

Another potential risk related to tariffs is the adverse scenario of a resurgence of inflation. However, Fibra UNO is resilient to high inflation thanks to the inflation-indexed nature of all its leases. Therefore, if inflation increases significantly due to excessive tariffs, Fibra UNO will be much more defensive than most other companies.

Final Thoughts

Fibra UNO has outperformed the S&P 500 and its American peers in recent months, primarily thanks to its resilience to the deteriorating economic landscape amid excessive tariffs. As long as the economic outlook remains uncertain, the REIT is likely to continue outperforming the broad market. On the other hand, if the ongoing financial turmoil is followed by a severe recession, the REIT will probably have to cut its dividend, given its high debt load. Overall, thanks to its strong business model and its superior resilience (vs. most companies) to a potential recession, Fibra UNO maintains its hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: March PPI: Tariffs Push Up Intermediate Goods Costs
data.description:
Following the cold CPI release yesterday, the headline PPI fell more than expected in March, recording the largest decline in more than a year. The final demand indexes were heavily affected by energy prices and softening consumer demand, with little impact from tariffs. On the other hand, the intermediate demand indexes did show evidence that higher import duties have already pushed up costs in the early stages of the supply chain.

Headline PPI

The headline PPI recorded a decline of -0.4% MoM in March, well below expectations of an increase of 0.2% MoM and the largest decline since October 2023. The annual rate of the index cooled for the second straight month, down to 2.7% YoY, the lowest since September 2024. The decline in the index was driven by two segments:

  • Just as we saw energy have a significant impact on the CPI release yesterday, the energy index played a major role in the PPI release. The final demand energy index was down -4.0% MoM in March and, like the headline MoM move, it was the largest decline since October 2023. The BLS points out that “over three-fourths of the March decline is attributable to [the] -4.0% drop in the index for final demand energy.” More specifically, it attributes two-thirds of the decline to the -11.1% MoM drop in gas prices.

  • Within the services PPI segment, there was a large decline in the index tracking trade services (-0.7% MoM) which is associated with margins in wholesale and retail trade. This followed a large -1.1% MoM decline in February and caused the annual increase to fall to 3.1% YoY, the lowest since November 2024. The margin compression in March likely came as a result of the decline in consumer prices in March and doesn’t necessarily point to a decline in costs for producers.

Final Demand PPI

While the energy and trade services indexes played a major role in the decline in the PPI index in March, cost growth broadly was soft. Final demand PPI excluding foods, energy, and trade services was only up 0.1% MoM in March, slowing from three straight increases of 0.4% MoM. On an annual basis, the aggregate was up 3.4% YoY, down from 3.5% YoY and the lowest since June 2024.

One of the softest final demand segments in March (not including energy and trade services) was transportation and warehousing services. It fell -0.6% MoM, the largest decline since August 2024. The drop was caused by a large -3.9% MoM move in passenger transportation costs that is similar to the decline in airfares seen in the March CPI report. This was partially offset by a rise of 0.5% MoM in the cost of transporting foods which has a slightly higher weight. It’s likely that the decline in passenger transport costs was a result of lower fuel costs and weaker demand for travel as consumers backed off of discretionary spending.

Did the early tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China impact the final demand indexes? It doesn’t look like there was much of a reaction. The March data for final demand appears to have been more impacted by the weakness in consumer prices and consumer spending. This is most strongly supported by the move in trade services PPI. It is possible that the slight rise in goods transport and warehousing costs may be an early reflection of tariff costs entering the supply chain, but that is unclear.

Intermediate PPI

While the final demand indexes did not show much of an impact from the early tariffs, there were more interesting moves in the intermediate PPI indexes. These indexes are different from the PPI readings that make headlines as they show the change in costs earlier in the supply chain. Here are some intermediate segments to note in today’s release:

  • The PPI for materials and components for manufacturing jumped 1.0% MoM in March, the largest monthly increase since May 2022. A string of strong increases in Q1 2025 sent the index up 1.3% QoQ, the highest since Q2 2022. The new trend in the last three months suggests that this increase is at least somewhat linked to tariffs. Within the index, materials for durable manufacturing increased 1.4% MoM in February and 3.1% MoM in March. These unusually high gains likely represent a sudden increase in costs for the materials needed for auto & other transport goods manufacturing.

  • Data for March suggested that there was also an increase in materials and components required for construction. That index increased 0.7% MoM in March which is the largest rise since May 2022 as well. With Canada being a major source of lumber and Mexico a major source of gypsum, tariffs on these goods likely drove the increase seen here.
  • The BLS discusses some details of the rise in intermediate processed goods costs: “Among prices for processed goods for intermediate demand in March, the index for steel mill products rose 7.1% MoM. Prices for structural, architectural, and pre-engineered metal products; primary basic organic chemicals; industrial electric power; and plastic resins and materials also moved higher.” These were all offset by a -11.1% drop in gasoline prices.
Goods Inputs PPI by Production Stage Inputs for Goods Producers Inputs for Services Producers
Stage 1: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +1.3% MoM +0.4% MoM
Stage 2: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +0.8% MoM +0.2% MoM
Stage 3: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +1.5% MoM +0.8% MoM
Stage 4: Goods (ex-Food, Energy) +0.5% MoM +0.3% MoM

The BLS breaks down PPI for intermediate goods by production stage. This breakdown shows that the largest increase in costs was earlier on in the supply chain (stage 1 is the earliest, and stage 4 is closest to final demand), and demonstrates the early impact of tariffs on US producers. It’s likely that these costs will continue to be pushed down the supply chain and eventually reach consumers.

Market Reaction

Once again, the markets were a bit distracted by headlines surrounding tariffs to have a big reaction to the PPI release. The largest move was in short-term yields as the United States 2-Year Bond Yield (US2Y) fell by about 2 bps. The United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) managed a minor downward move as well, but it was immediately reversed. The PPI headline move was a bit misleading because of the large impact of the energy index. The details in the intermediate indexes are more interesting and paint a more inflationary picture.

Overall, the March PPI report is summarized as a juxtaposition of an acceleration in intermediate goods cost growth and weakness in retail margins and a few other categories like passenger transportation costs that are related to softer demand. This also summarizes the balance of risks in the Fed’s inflation outlook that FOMC members will have to consider at the May meeting. The data today tempers my belief that the Fed can cut in May after yesterday’s cold CPI release, but it does not reverse it.

data.title: Stock Market Crash Does Not Foretell Recession Or Future Decline
data.description:
This time could be different, but this article will review data that includes the Cuban Missile Crisis, the Vietnam War, the inflationary 1970s, the double-dip recessions of the early 1980s, the Dot-Com Bubble and Covid-19. Every event is unique, so no one can be certain how the economy and the stock market will respond to the new tariffs. However, we have enough evidence to recommend chilling out.

A Stock Market Crash And Recession Risk

In 2022, I reviewed whether stock market drops foretold recessions. Using a 20% decline as a threshold for a bear market, here’s what I found. “The stock market did not drop this much from its previous peak around the recessions of 1953, 1957, 1960, 1980, 1981 or 1990. The stock market turned bear at the same time that the 1970 and 2020 recessions began, so it provided no early alert. It provided one month of advance notice for the 2001 recession. The stock market was three months late in its warning of the 1973 recession and eight months late in 2008. False warnings came in 1962, 1977 and 1987.”

Why would stocks drop 20% if not for a recession? A math example explains it. In the standard Dividend Discount Model, a stock is worth the sum of future dividends, discounted for the time value of money. A one dollar dividend, with a ten percent interest rate and a five percent expected growth rate, generates a stock market value of $20.00. If the stock analyst becomes pessimistic about the growth rate and cuts expectations for future growth from 5.00% down to 3.75%, the valuation of the stock goes from $20.00 to $16.00. A 20% drop in stock prices is the usual definition of a bear market. It can occur if long-term growth expectations are less optimistic, even if nobody expects a recession.

The key concept of the stock market is that today’s change reflects the change in expectations. A market decline does not necessarily imply that people are thinking the economy will get worse; they might simply think that past expectations were too optimistic.

Stock Market Crashes And Future Stock Prices

If a stock market decline does not forecast recession, does it at least forecast further stock market drops? Not at all. I looked at all instances since 1950 (when my data begin) of the Standard & Poor’s 500 index dropping at least as much as it did on April 4, six percent. There were 19 days of such a drop before the tariff-induced decline. I calculated the percent change in the index on subsequent days.

In the fifth day following a steep market drop, prices had increased 13 out of 19 times, achieving an average gain of three percent. Over the 30 days following the steep drop, again the market was up 13 out of 19 times, with an average rebound of four percent - not fully recovering, but moving up.

A full year after the day of steep decline, the market rose 16 out of 19 times, averaging a 24% rebound, which more than made up for the sharp drop. And ten years later, we had market gains in 15 out of 16 cases. (Three of the steep declines occurred during the 2020 pandemic, so we do not yet have 10-year results. As of this week, the market is 50% higher than its pre-pandemic peak.)

Will this year’s stock market drop be different? It certainly could be. Mr. Market offers no guarantees. But history suggests an implication for business leaders, and one for investors.

Business leaders should not assume that simply because of the stock market decline a recession is coming. But they should recognize that expectations for the future have changed. Perhaps people had been too exuberant in past months, or perhaps now they are unduly pessimistic. A person who had been highly optimistic when the market was booming in 2024 should now think of the market as a trusted friend who is saying, “Buddy, you’re ignoring important information.” But the person who watched last year’s stock market boom and thought, “Those kids on Wall Street are crazy,” can now feel more comfortable about that belief.

Investors who are panicked about their stocks should use this event to evaluate their investment strategy. They should not bail out of stocks simply because the market fell recently. Instead, they should consider their overall investment strategy. That includes investment goals, time horizon and tolerance for risk. Some people may determine that their strategy is fine and no changes are necessary. Others may want to adjust their asset allocation so that they sleep better at night. But it would be a mistake to sell stocks thinking, “I’ll get back into the market when it looks better.” It will, indeed, look better after it has gone up a good bit. At that time, the cautious investor has already missed the rebound.

Any asset allocation adjustment to reduce risk should be made with long-term goals in mind. A conservative investment strategy may not generate enough return over the long run for a comfortable retirement. If that’s the case, then trimming current expenses to increase savings should accompany the move to a more conservative portfolio. But if conservative investing will hit the goals and also provide psychological comfort, that’s a good approach.

We humans are emotional creatures. Whether that’s good or bad, it is who we are. But business and investment decisions are best made without emotions overwhelming us. A stock market crash can make us panicked in a way the hurts our decision-making. Stay calm and look at hard data.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oakmark Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
First-quarter highlights

Top contributors

  • American International Group (AIG)
  • Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)
  • Deere and Company (DE)

Top detractors

  • Alphabet Cl A (GOOG,GOOGL)
  • General Motors (GM)
  • Merck (MRK)

New purchases

  • Constellation Brands Cl A (STZ)
  • Marathon Petroleum (MPC)

Final sales

  • There were no final sales during the quarter.

FIRST QUARTER, 2025

  • The Fund’s Investor Share Class (OAKMX) returned 1.14% versus -4.27% for the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) for the quarter, and 12.76% since inception versus 10.44% for the index over the same period.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were financials and consumer staples, while consumer discretionary and communication services were the largest detractors.
  • As we have done in other periods when stock prices declined significantly, we are taking advantage of the opportunity to reposition the portfolio toward companies with the most attractive risk-adjusted returns.

TOP CONTRIBUTOR | DETRACTOR

Top contributor

American International Group (AIG) was the top contributor during the quarter. The company reported improved premium growth, strong underwriting results, and significant capital return during the period. We expect these trends to continue, which has the potential to lead to strong EPS (earnings per share) growth and improved returns. Over time, we expect the company’s return on equity to converge with peer levels. As returns continue to improve, we expect the valuation discount to the industry’s top peers to narrow.

Top detractor

Alphabet was the top detractor during the quarter. The U.S.-headquartered company’s stock price declined despite having posted fourth-quarter 2024 earnings that were in line with consensus expectations. Search revenue growth remained strong, and management reiterated that the new “AI Overviews” feature is driving higher engagement with comparable monetization. The one miss during the quarter was in the Cloud segment, where revenue grew 30% year-over-year but fell slightly short of consensus expectations. We believe the shortfall was largely due to short-term capacity constraints and that the long-term growth outlook for Google Cloud remains robust. We continue to see Alphabet as a collection of great businesses that can further benefit from the company’s world class AI capabilities. With shares trading at just 15x our estimate of next year’s earnings per share, we believe the stock is meaningfully undervalued.

PORTFOLIO ACTIVITY

New purchases

Marathon Petroleum is an integrated downstream energy company that refines, markets and transports petroleum products. Marathon commands the largest refining system in the United States with operations in attractive regions, which has provided meaningful cost advantages for the company and significant barriers to entry for competitors. In addition, we think Marathon's midstream business is an attractive asset thanks to its high market share in one of the most prolific gas fields in the world, which provides stability to the company's cash flows. Lastly, we appreciate management's focus on returns on invested capital and willingness to return capital to shareholders. Over the last year, refining industry margins have come under pressure due to a global wave of new supply during a period of soft demand. We believe industry margins have fallen below sustainable levels and that, in the long run, supply and demand will return to balance which should lead to higher refining margins for Marathon. Today’s short-term imbalance provided the opportunity to purchase shares at an attractive valuation relative to both current and mid-cycle earnings.

Constellation Brands is the leading imported beer company in the U.S. The company has a strong portfolio made up of iconic premium beer brands such as Modelo, Corona and Pacifico. We like that Constellation's beer segment has consistently outgrown the industry and believe it can further benefit from demographic tailwinds, new distribution points, and future price increases. More recently, the stock price has come under pressure due to both stockspecific and industry-wide challenges, some of which we believe will prove transitory. Despite the company’s strong historical performance and expectations for continued above-market beer growth, Constellation trades at a meaningful discount to other consumer packaged goods companies with similar growth outlooks. This dislocation afforded us the opportunity to initiate a position in the company at a significant discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

PORTFOLIO MANAGERS

(Year joined Harris | Oakmark)

Michael A. Nicolas, CFA (2013)

The price to earnings ratio (“P/E”) compares a company's current share price to its per-share earnings. It may also be known as the "price multiple" or "earnings multiple", and gives a general indication of how expensive or cheap a stock is. Investors should not base investment decisions on any single attribute or characteristic data point.

EPS refers to Earnings Per Share and is calculated by dividing total earnings by the number of shares outstanding.

The S&P 500 Index is a float-adjusted, capitalizationweighted index of 500 U.S. large-capitalization stocks representing all major industries. It is a widely recognized index of broad, U.S. equity market performance. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000® companies with lower price-tobook ratios and lower expected growth values. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility.

data.title: New Gold: A Copper-Heavy Future In A Golden World
data.description:
New Gold Inc. (NYSE:NGD) (TSX:NGD:CA) is a mid-tier gold producer, with two operational mines in the safe jurisdiction of Canada. Despite NGD being seemingly fair valued at present, I initiate coverage with a Strong Buy rating after its recent pull-back, for reasons that I will outline below.

Review of Operations

Rainy River Mine, Ontario, Canada

Rainy River commenced production as an open pit operation in 2017, and has a remaining life-of-mine of another four years (until 2028). However, underground mining at the same site, which began in 2022 and also produces silver as a by-product, is set to continue until 2033. Total gold production for Rainy River is set to peak in 2026, before declining thereafter (with silver production forecast to peak a year later, in 2027).

Historical Production Metrics

2024 gold production of 225,694 ounces was below guidance of 230koz–240koz, which management attributes to lower ore grades (which was partially offset by higher tonnes processed and higher gold recovery).

Despite a slightly disappointing operational performance in 2024 (which included unexpected mechanical down-time on the crushing and conveying system in Q4), Rainy River saw record unit margins in 2024, aided by high gold prices and silver sales, which helped lower AISC per equivalent ounce. AISC per equivalent ounce was just US$1,525/oz.

2025 Guidance

Management forecasts that 2025 gold production will be 20% higher due to a 25% increase in gold grade—with the underground mining rate expected to ramp up. Furthermore, in the Q4 2024 MD&A document, management remarked that production in 2025 will be higher in the second half of the year than in H1:

Production is expected to significantly strengthen in the second half of the year as significant waste stripping activities are sequenced in the first quarter. The first half of 2025 is expected to represent approximately 37% of the annual production, with the first quarter expected to represent approximately 11%. Ore from the low-grade stockpile will be processed in the first quarter as Phase 4 stripping during the first quarter will set up the open pit for low strip, high ore extraction for the balance of this phase. After the first quarter, the remaining 2025 average Phase 4 strip ratio is expected to be approximately 1:1, increasing production for the balance of the year. Production in the second half of the year is expected to be consistent between the third and fourth quarter.

New Afton Mine, British Columbia, Canada

New Afton is a gold-silver-copper mine that reached commercial production in 2012 and has a life-of-mine of 7 years (to 2031). According to the latest technical report, gold, silver and copper production is forecast to peak in 2027.

Historical Production Metrics

2024 gold production of 72,609 ounces exceeded management's guidance of 60koz - 70koz.

The higher gold production in 2024 was due to higher tonnes processed, partially offset by lower ore grades and recovery.

2024 copper production of 54 million pounds was near the mid-point of management's guidance of 50 million to 60 million pounds.

Despite higher copper production, ore grades and recovery were also lower than the prior year.

Significantly, the increased revenue from copper in 2024 led to negative AISC per gold equivalent ounce produced.

2025 Guidance

2025 gold and copper production are expected to be in line with 2024. In the Q4'24 MD&A document, management remark that production is expected to be higher in the second half of the year, and guided that AISC will be lower in 2025:

Production is expected to significantly strengthen in the second half of the year as the B3 cave is exhausted and C-Zone continues its ramp-up. The first half of 2025 is expected to represent approximately 45% of the annual gold and copper production, with the first quarter expected to represent approximately 20%. All-in sustaining costs (on a by-product basis) are expected to decrease compared to the 2024 midpoint of guidance to between ($625) and ($525) per ounce due to lower total cash costs, higher production and lower sustaining capital. Total cash costs and all-in sustaining costs, are expected to decrease on a quarterly basis throughout 2025 as throughput increases as C-Zone continues to ramp up.

My Thoughts On New Gold Inc.

I follow many stocks in the gold sector, and New Gold Inc is certainly one of my favorite picks over the medium term. In considering the long-term production guidance for both mines in New Gold's portfolio, the next three years appear particularly promising - with production expected to tail off after that. Despite New Gold Inc. having some operational risk related to having just two mines, I like the fact that both mines are in the safe jurisdiction of Canada.

I have a bullish view on all three metals that NGD is exposed to (Gold, Silver, and Copper). I am particularly excited by the increasing copper production at the New Afton mine, in this current price environment. The copper market is forecast to be in a 180k tonne deficit in 2025, and the average lead time for opening a new copper mine is 18 years. An article published on deVere Group's website reads:

The copper price is also benefitting from massive US imports as traders frontload purchases of the metal in anticipation of new tariffs from the Trump administration. The US relies on imported copper to fulfil around half of domestic demand for the metal. President Trump has identified copper as a strategic asset and his administration is currently investigating the feasibility of issuing tariffs on the commodity. In a recent report, analysts at ING said US copper purchases could cause tightening in the global market: “The US copper rush could leave the rest of the world tight on copper if demand picks up more quickly than expected.” They predicted the copper price would continue to benefit from the spectre of tariffs as the Trump administration’s investigation remained ongoing. Analysis by Trading Economics showed that recent copper shipments to the US have totalled around 500,000 tons per month, well above the monthly average of 70,000 tons. While copper is currently trading at a premium in the US, the surge of imports is tightening the market around much of the world and could accelerate price increases across the board.

Something that doesn't excite me about New Gold Inc. is that I do not yet see a clear long-term expansion plan. Rainy River has a remaining life-of-mine of 9 years, while New Afton has a remaining life-of-mine of 7 years. I would speculate that management's plan would be to make hay while the sun shines for the next three years at least (if commodity prices hold up), and to then look at purchasing another mine, potentially. In reading the MD&A document, I did not get comfort that exploration activities at either mine would amount to any exciting discoveries.

However, I did take comfort in management's clear focus on their medium-term expansion plan—evidenced by NGD's $164.6 million share issuance in May 2024 and a $100 million drawdown on its revolving credit facility—which was used to complete a partial buy-back of New Afton's free cash flow interest from the Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan.

Valuation

I prefer using forward EV/EBITDA ratios when valuing gold companies, as this guides me in understanding what I am paying for the underlying operations versus the earnings that they are forecasted to generate.

In selecting the peer group for my analysis, I have chosen gold miners that are between $1 billion and $4 billion in market cap (whilst excluding companies that derive a high proportion of their revenue from royalty or streaming contracts).

Understandably, many of these mid-cap producers are trading below their 5-year average FWD EV/EBITDA ratios. In this high gold price environment, many of these mid-tier miners have already mined their most accessible and profitable reserves. Investors are also more likely to favor larger, more stable gold producers with better cash flow visibility this late in the cycle—as they are likely to be better cushioned in the event of a gold price retreat.

Over the past five years, NGD traded at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 33% lower than its peers, on average. NGD is now trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that is just 2% undervalued versus its peers (while it is also just 2% undervalued versus its own 5-year FWD EV/EBITDA ratio)—suggesting that valuations have caught-up over this period.

My view is that it is completely warranted that valuations have caught-up versus the peer group, and I would happily take a position in NGD at the current price, as I wouldn't feel like I am overpaying.

Conclusion

New Gold Inc. gets my seal of approval. I expect that the company will perform well over the next three years, despite a lack of clarity over long-term prospects. I think that the recent pull-back in the stock presents an attractive entry point. I initiate coverage with a Strong Buy rating and have a $3.90 price target (representing at least 15% upside from current levels). I base my price target on the expectation that NGD's strong momentum will continue. With gold having reached a fresh all-time high today ($3,223/oz at the time of writing), NGD remains 10% below its recent peak—and I expect the stock to catch up and reach new all-time highs in the near future. However, I will be keeping my position small relative to the rest of my portfolio, as I am cognisant of higher risk associated with mid-tier gold producers—especially considering the strong rally they have already experienced.

data.title: Equinor Has A Top-Of-The-Line Profitability At Bottom-Of-The-Market Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Equinor (NYSE:EQNR) is Norway's energy giant that has both a strong presence in fossil fuel and renewables. It currently has a wide portfolio of Norwegian Continental Shelf assets plus other projects such as offshore wind, hydrogen, and CCs. The company has a smart strategy of funneling its substantial profits from O&G to green transition initiatives that are pushed by Norway.

My overall question would be: Is Equinor the best-positioned integrated oil company that could weather the current O&G market dynamic, and can it prove that it can correctly invest in the green transition?

Operation Overview

While initially focused on Norway's reserves, which account for a total of 7 billion barrels of proved oil reserves in 2023, the company has now grown into many operations in the energy sector worldwide, with the Norwegian state keeping a majority holding in Equinor. Outside of Norway, the company also has major projects in areas such as Brazil, the Gulf of Mexico, and the North Sea. It has become a leader in dealing with harsh offshore environments for E&P.

Norway has also become the largest gas provider for Europe, surpassing Russia, since the Ukraine invasion. Norway and Equinor were already in a prime position to capture the title before the invasion, as the country had already built an extensive network of subsea pipelines connecting to countries like Germany, France, and the U.K. Furthermore, Norway does not follow the norm like other "Petrostates," which have large reserves but limited export capacity, as Norway's domestic consumption is rather tiny and relies mostly on hydropower and other renewables for electricity.

Its legacy infrastructure has become a competitive advantage compared to LNG providers such as the U.S. and Qatar, which offer more expensive gas to offset the void that Russia left in Europe. Its proximity and long-standing contracts made Norway and, to the same extent, Equinor, a natural fit to take the crown of the largest energy provider. Norway now provides over 33% of all gas imports for EU members.

If we go deeper into Equinor's operation, the company separates its operation across 5 main categories: E&P Norway, E&P International, E&P USA, MMP, and REN.

E&P Norway represents the bulk of Equinor's operations, with around $24.6B in Adj. operating income for 2024. It englobes all of the upstream activities on the NCS and pumps roughly 1.4 million BOE/d, which accounts for roughly 70% of Norway's total O&G production.

E&P International is the segment that incorporates its Peregrino and Bacalhau fields in Brazil, Rosebank in the U.K., and many others. It is responsible for around 600,000 BOE/d of output with a mix of oil centered projects with some emerging gas plays. Production was negatively impacted by natural decline and temporary shutdowns, mainly in Brazil in 2024. That's why gas volumes decreased by around 16% compared to 2023, and that's why its Adj. Operating income decreased by around $810 million in 2024.

Daily production also decreased for E&P USA to the tune of 6% when looking at 2023. This is mainly due to lower efficiency for Caesar Tonga and many hurricanes impacting offshore assets. E&P USA posted a $1.03B Adj. operating income, with an average production of 341,000 BOE/d. E&P USA has assets in the Gulf of Mexico with Titan and Sparta (producing between 20,000-30,000 BOE/d and 50,000.70,000 BOE/d, respectively, but also keeps some onshore assets, such as in the Marcellus formation, where the company recently signed an agreement with EQT.

MMP focuses on trading, transporting, and refining Equinor's products. It handles around 2 million BOE/d in marketed products and acts as the bridge between Equinor's output and global markets. It has some refineries across Norway and many pipelines and terminals across Northern Europe. Its trading operation acts as a hedge in volatile markets when spreads are high. The segment generated $2.61B in Adj. operating income.

And finally, Renewables or REN, which encompasses the development and operation of all the company's renewable energy projects, which are primarily offshore wind. At the moment, there are around 4-5 GW in operation or under construction, with a target of 12–16 GW by 2030. Equinor aims for 50% of CapEx by 2030 in order to reach its net-zero 2050 goal. It established Hywind Scotland, a pioneer in a floating wind farm, which provides deepwater viability. However, high costs and grid delays are slowing profitability in the segment, which pushed a strategic reset in 2024.

Market Dynamics

Europe's gas demand remains robust, especially with the mentioned Russian gas phase out, but also with coal taking less and less shares of the energy market. However, Brent prices have dropped significantly this year due to the current market volatility related to the Trump administration. Although Equinor still has some leeway as it estimated that the company would be net cash flow neutral between 2025 and 2027 if prices stay at $50/bbl. European gas prices are remaining elevated compared to the pre-pandemic era, which also works in Equinor's favor.

Valuation

I'll start by saying that Equinor is cheap compared to its selected peers and the broader industry. It currently has an A grade for valuation by Seeking Alpha, which is heavily skewed by its bottom-of-the-market EV/EBITDA ratio of 1.83, around 70% lower than the sector median. That's in part thanks to its market-leading EBITDA margins, but also its strong cash and short-term investments stockpile of $23.5B. Its P/E is relatively low at 7.33 compared to the Sector Median of 11.04.

Equinor's profitability is also leading its peers, with some of the strong returns metrics I saw in the O&G industry. Now the question is, how come the company is not better valued amid these multiples? Well, as mentioned earlier, the company is majorly owned by the Norwegian government, which can direct Equinor in any direction. That's why Equinor is funneling so much money towards costly renewable projects, all the while keeping such a large dividend yield of 12.7% that goes directly back to the country. This could further restrain the company's growth prospects in the O&G sector.

Equinor is beating both XOM and CVX amid their scale and better legislative backdrop, which is certainly impressive. I don't understand how this company has become so cheap amid its geographical importance and its top profitability metrics.

Risks

Equinor's operation is dependent on inherently volatile commodity prices, which in today's day and age can certainly swing against the company. I believe that Equinor's trading desk can handle some volatile markets, but Brent has been dropping more and more towards Equinor's break-even prices, as it now stands at $59. Europe's gas reliance and its long-term contracts offer some buffers, yet Asia's LNG shift and U.S. shale could also flood markets, slashing prices.

As mentioned earlier, Norway is a 66% owner of Equinor, making the company dependent on the country's policies for its direction towards heavy renewable uses. Furthermore, the government's budget is dependent on Equinor's dividend, which would hinder any cuts despite deteriorating fundamentals.

Conclusion

Equinor is simply the best option for integrated Oil & Gas stocks. It is cheap and has strong fundamentals, with its massive competitive advantage of being the de facto gas supplier for Europe. I would rate Equinor a strong buy despite falling crude prices.

data.title: 10-Year U.S. Treasury Yield 'Fair Value' Estimate: 11 April, 2025
data.description:
The “fair value” for the US 10-year Treasury yield edged higher in March vs. the previous month and is now approaching the 4.0% mark – the highest estimate in 16 years. The analysis is based on the average of three models run by CapitalSpectator.com.

The current average monthly fair-value estimate is 3.91%, which remains below the actual 10-year yield. In yesterday’s trading, the benchmark rate rose to 4.44% (Apr. 10).

As discussed on these pages in recent history, a market premium for the 10-year rate has persisted since late-2022. The latest increase in the average fair-value estimate suggests that the actual 10-year yield will remain relatively steady if not trend higher in the near term.

History reminds that the market prices in an evolving run of premiums and discounts relative to the fair-value estimate, as shown by the spread in the chart below.

The current premium has pulled back from a relatively high level recently, but looks set to continue in positive terrain for the near term. Several factors are expected to maintain the market premium, including renewed inflation risk related to a global trade war.

Consumer inflation eased in March, but the data does not yet reflect the higher prices that are expected to arrive due to increases in US import tariffs.

“I think it’ll take some time for the inflationary shock to work its way into the system,” said Preston Caldwell, chief US economist at Morningstar. “At first, [inflation data] might look better than it will be eventually.”

The softer CPI data for last month “is good news for the Fed, but the data seems stale,” advised US Bank chief economist Beth Ann Bovino.

An opposing macro force that could reduce the market premium by driving the 10-year yield lower: slower growth if not recession, a risk factor that’s also heightened by higher tariffs.

The key question is whether inflation risk or slow growth/recession risk dominate for the 10-year yield’s path ahead. At the moment, it appears that the two risks are more or less balanced as the market churns in a range vs. recent history.

The wide swings in the 10-year yield in recent days suggest that investors are struggling to price in which risk is the bigger threat – inflation vs. slower growth/recession. A breakout above the recent peak (roughly 4.80%), or below the recent trough (3.90%), would signal a consensus in the crowd’s ex ante risk evaluation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dan Ivascyn On Bond Investing In A Turbulent Global Market
data.description:
Transcript

Dan Ivascyn: You mentioned a lot of volatility in equity markets and credit spreads have widened, a bit. Credit spreads have widened a bit. But you have very, very strong technicals, arguably still a little bit of complacency or supply demand imbalances. They're keeping spreads tight. That's an area which we may talk about more, but where we remain more cautious. But bonds, and I think it's important to put this into perspective, because the last few days have been rough.

But the ten-year Treasury yields back to the levels that we saw in mid-February. Just a few quarters ago, we had higher levels. And then the 30-year bonds retraced back to a level generally near where we were in January.

So it's been volatile. But we still have a situation where treasury yields, although they're high, they haven't become extremely dislocated yet. But again, that's an area that we're focusing on that you asked about the path for fixed income, as we've been saying time and time again. Yeah, it's rough. Yes, it can be a bumpy road.

But starting yields in the higher quality areas of the fixed income markets are attractive. Even if inflation were to be sustained comfortably above central bank targets. So this sell off has again created opportunity without having to lock up your money for an extended period of time, without having to take a lot of economically sensitive risk.

And again, it looks attractive absolute and relative to other segments of the market.

Disclosure

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. data.title: Hochschild Mining: Printing Cash At $3,100 Gold
data.description:
Introduction

As mentioned in my previous article on Hochschild Mining (OTCQX:HCHDF) (OTCPK:HCHDY), this is the perfect time to get a new gold mine up and running. Hochschild has now completed the construction of a new mine in Brazil which will contribute approximately 100,000 ounces of gold to this year’s production numbers. With gold above $3100 and the futures, indicating a price north of $3300/oz next year, the timing works well for Hochschild.

This company has its primary listing on the London Stock Exchange, so I'd strongly recommend to trade in the company's shares on that exchange, given the superior liquidity. The ticker symbol on the LSE is HOC and the average daily volume is approximately 2.8 million shares per day.

Ending the year on a strong note, thanks to the metal prices

During 2024, the company produced approximately 8.5 million ounces of silver as well as just under 250,000 ounces of gold compared to the preceding year. This represents an 11% decrease for the silver production and a 32% increase in the gold production. Given the very strong gold price lately, it’s nice to see the company being able to immediately benefit from that, and that was immediately noticeable in the financial results. Hochschild reported a 37% revenue increase and as the operating expenses increased at a slower pace, the adjusted EBITDA increased by in excess of 50%.

The all-in sustaining costs increased as well, and Hochschild’s published 2024 AISC was $1638 per ounce gold-equivalent which was about $100/oz higher than the previously issued cost guidance. This was related to a slower ramp-up than anticipated at the Mara Rosa mine, while the inflation in Argentina also played a role in the performance of the 51% owned San Jose mine.

The total reported revenue in 2024 was approximately $948M, which allowed Hochschild to report a gross profit of $342M, an increase of almost 85% compared to the preceding year.

As the income statement above shows, Hochschild reported an EBIT of just under $208M. That’s a very strong improvement over the FY 2023 result, as the amount of impairment expenses was much higher in 2023.

Looking at the bottom-line result, the reported net profit was almost $114M of which $97M was attributable to Hochschild’s shareholders with almost $17M attributable to non-controlling interests. This includes a net impact of almost $20M on the Hochschild level, and the income statement above does a good job in highlighting these non-recurring items. Adjusted for those items, Hochschild’s attributable net profit was almost $117M, representing approximately $0.23 per share.

Just like in the previous article, I’m very interested in the company’s cash flow profile. Before jumping to Hochschild’s cash flow statement, I wanted to start with one of the footnotes considering the company’s cash flow statement starts with the $365M in ‘cash generated from operations’. The details on how Hochschild achieved that result can be found below.

As you notice, the $365M in cash from operations includes a $29M investment in the working capital position, and that should be added back to the equation to find the normalized cash flows.

On the other hand, we should deduct the $5M in lease payments, and we should deduct the $55M in additional taxes that were part of the income statement but haven’t been paid out in cash yet.

This results in an adjusted operating cash flow of approximately $290M, and approximately $280M after taking the impact of the non-controlling interests into account.

As shown above, the total capex was approximately $234M (and this, of course, also still includes the remainder of the growth capex spent at Mara Rosa). About $20M of the capex was related to an intangible investment in legal rights (related to the permitting for exploration, development and production). Excluding that intangible investment, Hochschild generated about $76M in free cash flow. That doesn’t sound impressive, but let’s not forget the net realized price was just under $2350/oz for the gold. As market prices have increased, we can expect the average realized price to increase as well.

What can we expect in 2025?

The company also provided its outlook for this year, and Hochschild expects to produce 350,000-378,000 ounces of gold-equivalent thanks to the important contribution from the new Mara Rosa gold mine, which will produce about 100,000 ounces of gold this year. The Inmaculada mine in Peru had an exceptionally strong 2024 with an attributable production of just over 220,000 ounces of gold, but this isn’t sustainable, and the 2025 production guidance assumes a production decrease of 7-8%.

The all-in sustaining cost is estimated at $1587-1687/oz, so at the current gold price, the margin will be around $1300/oz using a $1700/oz AISC. Multiplied by the lower end of the production guidance, results in a net cash flow of $455M at the mine level.

Of course, we still have to deduct the net finance expenses (estimated at $15M) and the tax (approximately $125-140M) resulting in approximately $300M in normalized sustaining free cash flow. Of course, Hochschild will also expense money on exploration and pursue other growth projects, while there will be G&A on the corporate level as well. But taking these elements into account, at $3000 gold, I anticipate the net free cash flow to come in at around $225-250M.

Given the current share count of 514.5M shares outstanding, this would represent approximately $0.42 per share after also taking the cash flow and income attributable to non-controlling interests into account. This represents approximately 33 pence per share, which means the current share price of 277 pence still provides some upside potential.

Investment thesis

Hochschild’s share price has performed very well since my previous article, and I think the ‘easy gains’ have been made. I still see upside potential, so investors looking to retain exposure to the gold and silver prices may retain a long position or a portion of their long position.

It goes without saying the markets are pretty volatile these days, and it probably wouldn’t be wise to chase the stock, but every day the company can sell gold at $3100+ per ounce is a good day. I currently have no position in Hochschild but given the current strength in the gold price I should perhaps go long again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
